Session 1 — Exploring the Yogurt Dataset (Group A)

Overview

This document loads and explores all four CSV files in the yogurt study dataset (data/group_a_yogurt/). For each file we’ll show:

  • What one row represents (the unit of observation)
  • What every column means
  • A quick glance at the data
Code 
library(tidyverse)

00 — Sample IDs (00_sample_ids_yogurt.csv)

Code 
sample_ids <- read_csv("../data/group_a_yogurt/00_sample_ids_yogurt.csv")
glimpse(sample_ids)
Rows: 102
Columns: 4
$ pid        <chr> "pid_25", "pid_51", "pid_33", "pid_13", "pid_37", "pid_09",…
$ time_point <chr> "baseline", "baseline", "baseline", "baseline", "after_anti…
$ arm        <chr> "unchanged_diet", "yogurt", "yogurt", "yogurt", "unchanged_…
$ sample_id  <chr> "UNCH001", "YOG002", "YOG003", "YOG004", "UNCH005", "UNCH00…

What one row represents:
A single biological sample taken from one participant at one time point.

Column guide:

Column Type Meaning
pid identifier Participant ID. Links to 01_participant_metadata_yogurt.csv.
time_point categorical When the sample was taken — baseline (before antibiotics) or after_antibiotic (after a course of antibiotics).
arm categorical Study arm — yogurt (ate yogurt during antibiotics) or unchanged_diet (no yogurt, normal diet).
sample_id identifier Unique sample code (e.g. UNCH001 or YOG002). Links to qPCR and luminex tables.
Code 
sample_ids |>
  count(arm, time_point) |>
  knitr::kable(col.names = c("Arm", "Time Point", "n"))
Arm Time Point n
unchanged_diet after_antibiotic 25
unchanged_diet baseline 25
yogurt after_antibiotic 26
yogurt baseline 26

01 — Participant Metadata (01_participant_metadata_yogurt.csv)

Code 
metadata <- read_csv("../data/group_a_yogurt/01_participant_metadata_yogurt.csv")
glimpse(metadata)
Rows: 51
Columns: 7
$ pid                 <chr> "pid_01", "pid_02", "pid_03", "pid_04", "pid_05", …
$ arm                 <chr> "unchanged_diet", "unchanged_diet", "unchanged_die…
$ days_since_last_sex <dbl> 2, 4, 3, 1, 5, 9, 3, 4, 10, 7, 9, 20, 1, 1, 11, 3,…
$ birth_control       <chr> "no hormonal birth control", "Depoprovera", "no ho…
$ age                 <dbl> 28, 34, 32, 30, 34, 27, 25, 26, 34, 32, 32, 32, 30…
$ education           <chr> "grade 10-12, not matriculated", "less than grade …
$ sex                 <chr> "female", "female", "female", "female", "female", …

What one row represents:
One study participant (51 participants total).

Column guide:

Column Type Meaning
pid identifier Participant ID. Links to 00_sample_ids_yogurt.csv.
arm categorical Study arm — yogurt or unchanged_diet.
days_since_last_sex numeric Days since the participant last had sex (range 1–48).
birth_control categorical Birth control method — Depoprovera (injectable contraceptive) or no hormonal birth control.
age numeric Age in years (range 24–37).
education categorical Highest education level — less than grade 9, grade 10-12, not matriculated, grade 10-12, matriculated, or post-secondary.
sex categorical All participants are female.
Code 
metadata |>
  count(arm, birth_control) |>
  knitr::kable(col.names = c("Arm", "Birth Control", "n"))
Arm Birth Control n
unchanged_diet Depoprovera 8
unchanged_diet no hormonal birth control 17
yogurt Depoprovera 17
yogurt no hormonal birth control 9
Code 
metadata |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean_age = mean(age),
    min_age = min(age),
    max_age = max(age)
  )
# A tibble: 1 × 4
      n mean_age min_age max_age
  <int>    <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>
1    51     30.7      24      37

02 — qPCR Results (02_qpcr_results_yogurt.csv)

Code 
qpcr <- read_csv("../data/group_a_yogurt/02_qpcr_results_yogurt.csv")
glimpse(qpcr)
Rows: 102
Columns: 4
$ sample_id      <chr> "UNCH092", "UNCH042", "UNCH015", "UNCH035", "UNCH019", …
$ qpcr_bacteria  <dbl> 3093064.20, 8275.04, 7532202.40, 4794320.40, 5328343.60…
$ qpcr_crispatus <dbl> 0.000, 0.000, 0.000, 12.994, 0.000, 0.000, 0.000, 38.58…
$ qpcr_iners     <dbl> 26.840, 76.040, 554.964, 204308.968, 107806.148, 121045…

What one row represents:
Quantitative PCR (qPCR) measurements of bacterial DNA from one vaginal swab sample (102 samples total — one row per sample).

Column guide:

Column Type Meaning
sample_id identifier Links to 00_sample_ids_yogurt.csv.
qpcr_bacteria numeric Total bacterial load (16S rRNA gene copies).
qpcr_crispatus numeric Amount of Lactobacillus crispatus DNA — a “good” bacterium that helps protect against infections.
qpcr_iners numeric Amount of Lactobacillus iners DNA — another Lactobacillus species, but less protective than L. crispatus.
Code 
# Quick look at the range of bacterial loads
qpcr |>
  summarise(
    min_bacteria = min(qpcr_bacteria),
    median_bacteria = median(qpcr_bacteria),
    max_bacteria = max(qpcr_bacteria),
    pct_detectable_crispatus = mean(qpcr_crispatus > 0) * 100,
    pct_detectable_iners = mean(qpcr_iners > 0) * 100
  ) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 1)
min_bacteria median_bacteria max_bacteria pct_detectable_crispatus pct_detectable_iners
4251.7 2236996 126400280 35.3 92.2

03 — Luminex Cytokine Results (03_luminex_results_yogurt.csv)

Code 
luminex <- read_csv("../data/group_a_yogurt/03_luminex_results_yogurt.csv")
glimpse(luminex)
Rows: 1,020
Columns: 4
$ sample_id <chr> "UNCH092", "UNCH092", "UNCH092", "UNCH092", "UNCH092", "UNCH…
$ cytokine  <chr> "IL-1a", "IL-10", "IL-1b", "IL-8", "IL-6", "TNFa", "IP-10", …
$ conc      <dbl> 5.8500, 0.7670, 0.2505, 7.9200, 0.0935, 0.4710, 177.3800, 36…
$ limits    <chr> "within limits", "out of range", "out of range", "within lim…
Code 
n_distinct(luminex$sample_id)  # how many unique samples
[1] 102

What one row represents:
One cytokine measurement from one sample. The data is in “long” format — every sample has 10 rows, one per cytokine. (102 samples × 10 cytokines = 1,020 rows.)

Column guide:

Column Type Meaning
sample_id identifier Links to 00_sample_ids_yogurt.csv.
cytokine categorical Immune signalling protein measured: IL-1a, IL-1b, IL-6, IL-8, IL-10, TNFa, IFNg (listed as IFN-Y), IP-10, MIG, MIP-3a.
conc numeric Concentration of the cytokine (pg/mL).
limits categorical Reliability flag — within limits (reliable reading) or out of range (concentration too low or too high for the assay to measure accurately).
Code 
# Which cytokines are measured
luminex |>
  count(cytokine) |>
  knitr::kable()
cytokine n
IFN-Y 102
IL-10 102
IL-1a 102
IL-1b 102
IL-6 102
IL-8 102
IP-10 102
MIG 102
MIP-3a 102
TNFa 102
Code 
# Proportion of out-of-range results per cytokine
luminex |>
  mutate(flagged = limits != "within limits") |>
  group_by(cytokine) |>
  summarise(pct_flagged = mean(flagged) * 100) |>
  arrange(desc(pct_flagged)) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 1)
cytokine pct_flagged
IL-10 100.0
IFN-Y 99.0
TNFa 98.0
IL-6 75.5
IL-1b 72.5
MIP-3a 23.5
MIG 12.7
IL-8 3.9
IP-10 2.9
IL-1a 0.0

How the tables link together

All four tables are linked through key columns:

01_participant_metadata  ─── pid ───→ 00_sample_ids
                                            │
                                     sample_id
                                        ├──→ 02_qpcr_results    (1 row per sample)
                                        └──→ 03_luminex_results (10 rows per sample)

  • 01_participant_metadata ← pid → 00_sample_ids
    (one participant ↔︎ multiple samples: baseline + after antibiotic)

  • 00_sample_ids ← sample_id → 02_qpcr_results
    (one sample ↔︎ one qPCR row)

  • 00_sample_ids ← sample_id → 03_luminex_results
    (one sample ↔︎ 10 cytokine rows)

Code 
# Join all three data tables together for a complete picture
qpcr_with_metadata <- qpcr |>
  left_join(sample_ids, by = "sample_id") |>
  left_join(metadata, by = "pid", suffix = c(".sample", "")) |>
  select(sample_id, pid, time_point, arm, everything())

qpcr_with_metadata |>
  count(arm, time_point) |>
  knitr::kable()
arm time_point n
unchanged_diet after_antibiotic 25
unchanged_diet baseline 25
yogurt after_antibiotic 26
yogurt baseline 26

Arm vs. time point — total bacterial load

Code 
qpcr_with_metadata <- qpcr_with_metadata |>
  mutate(time_point = fct_relevel(time_point, "baseline", "after_antibiotic"))

# Total bacteria across time points, by study arm
qpcr_with_metadata |>
  ggplot(aes(x = time_point, y = qpcr_bacteria, fill = arm)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.6, outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(aes(color = arm), width = 0.15, alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(
    title = "Total bacterial load across time points",
    subtitle = "By study arm — yogurt vs. unchanged diet",
    x = "Time point",
    y = "Total bacteria (log scale)",
    fill = "Arm",
    color = "Arm"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

L. crispatus vs. L. iners — which species dominates?

Code 
# Add a small constant so we can plot zeros on a log scale
qpcr_with_metadata |>
  ggplot(aes(x = qpcr_crispatus + 1, y = qpcr_iners + 1, color = arm)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  scale_y_log10() +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  labs(
    title = "L. crispatus vs. L. iners across all samples",
    subtitle = "Dashed line = equal amounts. Points above it = more iners, below = more crispatus",
    x = "L. crispatus + 1 (log scale)",
    y = "L. iners + 1 (log scale)",
    color = "Arm"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Key cytokine — IL-1a across time points

Code 
# Join luminex data and pick IL-1a
luminex_plot <- luminex |>
  filter(cytokine == "IL-1a") |>
  left_join(sample_ids, by = "sample_id") |>
  left_join(metadata, by = "pid", suffix = c(".sample", "")) |>
  mutate(time_point = fct_relevel(time_point, "baseline", "after_antibiotic"))

luminex_plot |>
  ggplot(aes(x = time_point, y = conc, fill = arm)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.6, outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(aes(color = arm), width = 0.15, alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(
    title = "IL-1a concentration across time points",
    subtitle = "By study arm — yogurt vs. unchanged diet",
    x = "Time point",
    y = "IL-1a (pg/mL, log scale)",
    fill = "Arm",
    color = "Arm"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Quick notes

  • Study design: This is a longitudinal study following 51 participants at 2 time points — baseline (before antibiotics) and after_antibiotic. Half ate yogurt during their antibiotic course, the other half kept their normal diet.

  • Why yogurt? The study is testing whether eating yogurt (which contains live bacteria) helps restore a healthy vaginal microbiome after antibiotics disrupt it.

  • qPCR targets: The study measures Lactobacillus crispatus (a “good” species that produces protective lactic acid) and Lactobacillus iners (a related species that’s less protective). Many samples have zero L. crispatus — it was undetectable.

  • Birth control: About half the participants use Depoprovera (an injectable hormonal contraceptive), which can affect the vaginal microbiome.

  • Missing / flagged data: Luminex has out of range flags. Some cytokines (like IL-10, TNFa) are frequently out of range, meaning they’re too low to measure reliably in many samples.

  • Things we don’t know yet: What specific antibiotic was given? For how long? How much yogurt did the yogurt group eat? Were there any side effects?
